title: Ὄνος ἐνδυσάμενος λεοντῆν καὶ ἀλώπηξ
titleEn: The Donkey in the Lion's Skin and the Fox
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 267 (= Perry 188). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Ὄνος ἐνδυσάμενος λέοντος δορὰν περιῄει ἐκφοβῶν τὰ ἄλογα ζῷα.
sentences.tr: Onos endysamenos leontos doran periēiei ekphobōn ta aloga zōia.
sentences.l: A donkey, having-put-on a lion's skin, went-about terrifying the brute animals.
sentences.i: A donkey put on a lion's skin and went about terrifying the dumb animals.
sentences.n: τὰ ἄλογα ζῷα — 'the unreasoning animals,' the standard Greek term for beasts; the joke is that the donkey is one of them. The imperfect περιῄει makes the masquerade a career.

sentences.t: Καὶ δὴ θεασάμενος ἀλώπεκα ἐπειρᾶτο καὶ ταύτην δεδίττεσθαι.
sentences.tr: Kai dē theasamenos alōpeka epeirato kai tautēn dedittesthai.
sentences.l: And so, having-caught-sight-of a fox, he-tried also her to-frighten.
sentences.i: And catching sight of a fox, he tried to frighten her too.

sentences.t: Ἡ δὲ (ἐτύγχανε γὰρ αὐτοῦ φθεγξαμένου προακηκουῖα) ἔφη πρὸς τὸν ὄνον· « Ἀλλ' εὖ ἴσθι ὡς καὶ ἐγὼ ἄν σε ἐφοβήθην, εἰ μὴ ὀγκωμένου ἤκουσα. »
sentences.tr: Hē de (etynchane gar autou phthenxamenou proakēkouia) ephē pros ton onon: « All' eu isthi hōs kai egō an se ephobēthēn, ei mē onkōmenou ēkousa. »
sentences.l: But she (for she-happened of-him having-brayed to-have-heard-before) said to the donkey: « Well, know well that also I would-have-feared you, if not braying I-had-heard-you. »
sentences.i: But the fox — who, as it happened, had already heard him bray — said to the donkey: « Believe me, I'd have been frightened of you too, if I hadn't heard you braying. »
sentences.n: ἐτύγχανε … προακηκουῖα: τυγχάνω + participle, 'happened to have heard' — chance undoes the costume. ὀγκάομαι is the donkey's own verb, to bray; hearing beats seeing, and one syllable of voice defeats a whole lion's skin. εὖ ἴσθι again — the mouse's phrase to the lion.

sentences.t: Οὕτως ἔνιοι τῶν ἀπαιδεύτων τοῖς ἔξωθεν τύφοις δοκοῦντές τινες εἶναι ὑπὸ τῆς ἰδίας γλωσσαλγίας ἐλέγχονται.
sentences.tr: Houtōs enioi tōn apaideutōn tois exōthen typhois dokountes tines einai hypo tēs idias glōssalgias elenchontai.
sentences.l: So some of-the uneducated, by-the outward pomps seeming somebodies to-be, by the own tongue-itch are-exposed.
sentences.i: So some ignorant men, who pass for somebody by outward show, are exposed the moment their own tongues start itching.
sentences.n: γλωσσαλγία — literally 'tongue-ache': the itch to talk. δοκοῦντές τινες εἶναι, 'seeming to be somebodies' — τις as 'a somebody.' ἐλέγχονται is the philosopher's word for refutation: the donkey is refuted, Socratically, by his own voice.
